preFix.title: Dyeing with meadowsweet
preFix.subtitle: Cream to pale buff shades from Filipendula ulmaria flowers on alum-mordanted wool
preFix.excerpt: Meadowsweet (Filipendula ulmaria) is a common British hedgerow and riverside plant whose flowers give cream to pale buff-yellow on alum-mordanted wool. The colour is delicate; prolonged boiling fades it. An iron modifier shifts the result to a muted greenish-grey. Flowers can be used fresh or dried and are in season from June to August in the UK.
preFix.sourceNotes: Pellew, C.O., 'Dyes and Dyeing' (1913); traditional British hedgerow-dye practice. Alum percentages and temperature guidance consistent with UK natural dye guild instruction.

content.text: Meadowsweet (Filipendula ulmaria) grows along riverbanks, in damp meadows, and in hedgerows across Britain, flowering from June to August. The flowers, used fresh or dried, give cream to pale buff-yellow shades on alum-
mordanted
wool. Mordant at 15%
weight of fibre

(WOF) with alum potash. The colour is delicate and heat-sensitive: keep the dye bath below 80°C and do not prolong the hold time beyond 45 minutes, as higher heat or extended boiling fades the result. An
iron modifier
after-bath shifts the cream to a muted greenish-grey.
Lightfastness
is moderate: keep finished pieces away from prolonged direct sunlight to preserve the colour.

attrs.body: Work outdoors or with an extractor fan. Dedicated pots only. Gloves, apron, closed-toe shoes. Keep children and pets out of the dye area.
attrs.title: Mordant safety

items.name: Iron sulphate (optional, for after-bath colour shift)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Pre-wet the fibre

Soak the dry wool in warm water for at least 30 minutes until fully saturated. Every part of the skein or fleece must be wet through before it enters any bath. Lift gently to check for dry spots: any area that resists water will also resist colour. Set the pre-wetted fibre aside in a bowl of warm water.
Step 2: Mordant bath

Dissolve 15 g of alum potash in a cup of hot water, then add to the dye pot with 4 litres of water and stir. Lower the pre-wetted wool into the pot. Heat to 80°C over 30 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes. Hold at 80°C for 45 minutes. Remove the pot from the heat and allow the wool to cool in the mordant liquor for at least 20 minutes before lifting. The alum bath can be used again for a second mordanting session at a slightly lower dose.
Step 3: Dye-plant preparation

Place the meadowsweet flowers in a pot with 4 litres of cold water. Bring slowly to a simmer and hold at 75°C for 30 to 45 minutes. Do not boil: temperatures above 80°C cause the colour to fade in the extraction stage. Strain the plant material through a muslin cloth and discard the solids. Return the strained pale-gold liquor to the dye pot.
Step 4: Dye bath

Lower the mordanted wool into the strained dye liquor while both are at a similar temperature. Heat to 75°C and hold at 75°C for 45 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes. Do not push the temperature above 80°C. Remove the pot from the heat and let the wool cool in the bath for 30 minutes.
Step 5: Rinse and dry

Lift the wool from the dye bath and rinse in water close to the dye bath temperature, then rinse in progressively cooler water until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently, do not wring. Hang in a shaded spot to dry. Direct sunlight during drying will cause colour to fade even before the piece is complete.
Colour variations
Alum mordant produces a cream to pale buff-yellow. The result with fresh flowers leans slightly warmer than dried flowers, which give a purer cream.

Iron modifier: dissolve 2 g of iron sulphate in a cup of hot water and add to 2 litres of warm water in a separate pot. After the main dye bath, transfer the freshly dyed, still-warm wool to the iron bath and hold at 60°C for 15 minutes. The cream shifts to a muted greenish-grey. Rinse thoroughly after the iron bath.

Pour spent iron modifier bath down a foul drain only. Never pour into a storm drain, a stream, a garden soak-away, or any watercourse. Iron sulphate in accumulated quantities is harmful to aquatic life.
No mordant: on un-mordanted wool, meadowsweet flowers give a very pale cream that fades quickly with washing. The alum mordant makes a substantial difference to both colour depth and durability.
Troubleshooting

items.fix: Ensure the extraction simmers at 75°C for the full 30 to 45 minutes. Use 150 g of fresh flowers (or 75 g dried) per 100 g of dry fibre for a stronger result. Pale colour from meadowsweet is normal; it is a delicate dye rather than a strong one.
items.cause: The dye bath temperature was too low during extraction, or the hold time was too short
items.problem: The colour is very pale, almost white, on the dried fibre

items.fix: Always wash meadowsweet-dyed items in cool water (under 30°C) with a small amount of pH-neutral wool wash. Confirm the mordant bath held at 80°C for 45 minutes before dyeing. A second mordant session on the same fibre can improve fastness if the first result was disappointing.
items.cause: The mordant bath may not have held temperature long enough, or the wash water was too warm
items.problem: The colour fades noticeably after the first wash

items.fix: Use filtered or soft water for the dye bath. Alternatively, treat the cream result as a starting point and expect some local water variation in the final shade.
items.cause: The water supply is high in iron (common in some UK areas), which acts as an unintended iron modifier
items.problem: The colour turned greenish-brown in the dye bath rather than cream
